name: Magnetism and Matter
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. The Bar Magnet and Magnetic Field Lines
sections.body:
A bar magnet behaves as a magnetic dipole with two poles (north and south). Properties:

  • Like poles repel, unlike poles attract.
  • Magnetic poles always exist in pairs — magnetic monopoles do not exist.
  • A freely suspended magnet aligns roughly north-south (used in compass).

Magnetic field lines:

  • Form closed loops (unlike electric lines which terminate on charges).
  • Outside the magnet, run from N to S; inside, from S to N.
  • Tangent at any point gives field direction.
  • Density indicates field strength.
  • Never intersect.

sections.heading: 2. Bar Magnet as an Equivalent Solenoid
sections.body:
A bar magnet is equivalent to a current-carrying solenoid. Its magnetic dipole moment $\vec{m}$ points from S to N inside the magnet.

For a solenoid of $N$ turns, current $I$, length $2l$, area $A$: $m = NI(2l)A$. Field at axial distance $r$ from centre ($r \gg l$): $$B_{\text{axial}} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\dfrac{2m}{r^3}$$

Field on the equatorial line: $$B_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\dfrac{m}{r^3}$$ direction antiparallel to $\vec{m}$.

This mirrors the electric dipole field exactly with $1/4\pi\varepsilon_0 \to \mu_0/4\pi$ and $\vec{p}\to\vec{m}$.

sections.heading: 3. Magnetic Dipole in a Uniform Field
sections.body:
A magnetic dipole of moment $\vec{m}$ in a uniform field $\vec{B}$ experiences:

  • Net force = 0 (forces on N and S cancel).
  • Torque: $\vec{\tau} = \vec{m}\times\vec{B}$, magnitude $mB\sin\theta$, tending to align $\vec{m}$ with $\vec{B}$.
  • Potential energy: $U = -\vec{m}\cdot\vec{B} = -mB\cos\theta$. Minimum at $\theta = 0$ (stable), maximum at $\theta = \pi$ (unstable).

Work done in rotating dipole from $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$: $W = mB(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$.

For small oscillations of a dipole about equilibrium, period $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I/(mB)}$, where $I$ is moment of inertia.

sections.heading: 4. Earth's Magnetism
sections.body:
Earth behaves as a giant magnetic dipole tilted ~11.3° to its rotational axis, with the magnetic south pole near geographic north. The field at the surface is ~$3$–$6\times 10^{-5}$ T.

Three elements of Earth's magnetic field at any location:

  • Declination ($D$): angle between geographic and magnetic meridians.
  • Dip / inclination ($I$): angle between Earth's field and the horizontal.
  • Horizontal component ($B_H = B\cos I$). The vertical component is $B_V = B\sin I$. Total field: $B = \sqrt{B_H^2 + B_V^2}$, and $\tan I = B_V/B_H$.

At magnetic equator $I = 0$ ($B_V = 0$, $B = B_H$); at magnetic poles $I = 90°$ ($B_H = 0$).

sections.heading: 5. Magnetisation and Magnetic Intensity
sections.body:
Magnetisation $\vec{M}$: net magnetic moment per unit volume of a material. Unit: A/m.

Magnetic intensity $\vec{H}$: defined so that $\vec{B} = \mu_0(\vec{H} + \vec{M})$. In a linear material, $\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H}$, where $\chi_m$ is the magnetic susceptibility (dimensionless).

Permeability: $\mu = \mu_0(1 + \chi_m) = \mu_0\mu_r$, where $\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m$ is the relative permeability.

Then $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H}$. For vacuum, $\chi_m = 0$, $\mu = \mu_0$.

sections.heading: 6. Diamagnetism, Paramagnetism, Ferromagnetism
sections.body:
Diamagnetic ($\chi_m$ small, negative; $\mu_r < 1$): repelled weakly by magnets. Examples: bismuth, copper, water, gold. Origin: induced currents oppose external field (Lenz's law). Independent of temperature. Superconductors are perfect diamagnets ($\chi_m = -1$).

Paramagnetic ($\chi_m$ small, positive; $\mu_r > 1$): weakly attracted. Examples: aluminium, sodium, oxygen. Atoms have permanent magnetic moments that partially align with field. Curie's law: $\chi_m = C/T$.

Ferromagnetic ($\chi_m$ large, positive; $\mu_r \gg 1$, up to $10^4$): strongly attracted, retain magnetisation. Examples: iron, cobalt, nickel, gadolinium. Atoms align in domains; domain walls move under external field. Above the Curie temperature $T_C$, ferromagnets become paramagnetic.

sections.heading: 7. Hysteresis
sections.body:
When a ferromagnet is taken through a complete cycle of magnetisation, $B$ vs $H$ traces a closed loop — the hysteresis loop — not a single curve.

Key points on the loop:

  • Retentivity ($B_r$): residual magnetic field when $H = 0$ after saturation.
  • Coercivity ($H_c$): reverse $H$ required to reduce $B$ to zero.

Area of the loop = energy dissipated per unit volume per cycle.

Soft ferromagnets (low retentivity, low coercivity, narrow loop): ideal for transformer cores, electromagnets — easy to magnetise/demagnetise. Example: soft iron, mu-metal.

Hard ferromagnets (high retentivity and coercivity, fat loop): used for permanent magnets. Example: alnico, steel, neodymium alloys.

summary: A bar magnet is equivalent to a solenoid with moment $m$. Magnetic field lines form closed loops; monopoles don't exist. A dipole feels torque $\vec{m}\times\vec{B}$ and energy $-\vec{m}\cdot\vec{B}$ in external field. Earth's magnetism is described by declination, dip, and horizontal component. Materials are classified diamagnetic ($\chi_m < 0$), paramagnetic ($\chi_m > 0$, small), or ferromagnetic ($\chi_m \gg 0$). Ferromagnets show hysteresis with retentivity, coercivity.
pitfalls: Magnetic monopoles do not exist; field lines always form closed loops.
Earth's magnetic SOUTH pole is near geographic NORTH (that's why N pole of compass points north).
Diamagnetism is universal but masked by stronger para/ferromagnetism in those materials.
Curie's law applies to paramagnets; above Curie temperature, ferromagnets become paramagnetic.
Soft ferromagnets (narrow loop, low coercivity) for cores; hard (wide loop, high coercivity) for permanent magnets.
Magnetic flux is always ZERO through a closed surface (Gauss's law for magnetism).
